conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions.

First, let me review the official eligibility rules to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now I can help you determine your eligibility for Child Benefit.

To start, could you please tell me: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit and would like to know if I'm eligible. I have one child - my son Alex, who's 7 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have one child, Alex, who is 7 years old.

To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to ask you a few more questions.

**Do you live with Alex in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I live in the UK, but Alex doesn't live with me at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I understand. Alex doesn't live with you at the moment, but you're in the UK.

**Could you tell me who Alex is currently living with, and do you make any financial contributions towards his upkeep (such as for clothes, food, pocket money, or accommodation)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is living with his other parent. I do contribute towards his upkeep - I help with things like clothes, food, and other essentials he needs.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. It's helpful to know that you contribute towards Alex's upkeep even though he lives with his other parent.

**How much do you contribute towards Alex's upkeep, and how often do you make these contributions (for example, weekly or monthly)?** 

I ask this because to qualify for Child Benefit when a child lives with someone else, you need to contribute at least the same amount as the Child Benefit payment, which is currently around £25.60 per week for the eldest/only child.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I contribute £50 a week towards Alex's upkeep.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. You're contributing £50 per week, which is more than the Child Benefit amount.

One more important question: **Is Alex's other parent (who he lives with) currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

This is important because only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at any one time.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, his other parent isn't claiming Child Benefit for Alex at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me ask one final question to complete the assessment:

**Do you or your partner (if you have one) have an individual income over £60,000 per year?**

This doesn't affect your eligibility to claim Child Benefit, but it may mean you need to pay a tax charge on it.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'd rather not get into the income details right now - I'm mainly just trying to understand if I can actually claim the benefit for Alex first.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
That's absolutely fine - I have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me provide you with my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:
- Alex is 7 years old (under 16), so he meets the age requirement
- Even though Alex doesn't live with you, you're contributing £50 per week towards his upkeep, which is more than the Child Benefit payment amount
- According to the rules, you're considered responsible for a child if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them (on things like food, clothes, or pocket money)
- You live in the UK
- Alex's other parent is not currently claiming Child Benefit, and only one person can claim for the same child

You can proceed with making a claim for Child Benefit for Alex. Just to note for the future: if you or a partner have an individual income over £60,000, you may need to pay a tax charge on the Child Benefit, but you can still claim it.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about claiming Child Benefit?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 7 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 7 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
circumstances: Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 7 years old, which is under 16, so he meets the age requirement. Although Alex does not live with the claimant, the claimant is contributing £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which is more than the Child Benefit amount (approximately £25.60 per week for an eldest/only child). According to the eligibility rules, you'll usually be responsible for a child if you're paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards looking after them (for example on food, clothes or pocket money). The claimant lives in the UK, and Alex's other parent is not currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Since only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at any time, and no one else is claiming, the claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for their 7-year-old son Alex. Even though Alex lives with his other parent, the claimant contributes £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit amount. The other parent is not currently claiming Child Benefit, so there is no conflict. The claimant meets the responsibility test by making financial contributions at least equal to the Child Benefit payment.

The claimant lives in the UK, and Alex's other parent is not currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Since only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at any time, and no one else is claiming, the claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Even though Alex lives with his other parent, the claimant contributes £50 per week towards Alex's upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit amount. The other parent is not currently claiming Child Benefit, so there is no conflict. The claimant meets the responsibility test by making financial contributions at least equal to the Child Benefit payment.
